subject: 8C English Literature
title: In the Bazaars of Hyderabad — Sarojini Naidu

sections.title: About the Poet — Sarojini Naidu
sections.content:
Sarojini Naidu (1879–1949) was one of India's greatest poets, a freedom fighter, and a trailblazer for women in Indian politics. She was born in Hyderabad into a Bengali family. Her father, Aghornath Chattopadhyay, was a scientist and philosopher, and her mother, Barada Sundari Devi, was a poetess who wrote in Bengali.

Sarojini was a child prodigy — she passed the Madras University matriculation examination at the age of 12! She later studied at King's College London and Girton College Cambridge in England. It was during her time in England that she began writing poetry seriously.

She earned the sobriquet (nickname) 'The Nightingale of India' because of the lyrical beauty, rich imagery, and musical quality of her poems. Her poetry celebrates India's colours, sounds, traditions, and people.

Beyond poetry, Sarojini Naidu was a towering figure in India's independence movement. She was:
- The FIRST Indian woman to become President of the Indian National Congress (1925)
- The FIRST woman governor of an Indian state (Governor of Uttar Pradesh, 1947-1949)
- A close associate of Mahatma Gandhi in the freedom struggle

Her poems are famous for their depiction of the beauty of simple joys and sorrows of life, originality of theme, and harmony of rhythm and music. Her major poetry collections include 'The Golden Threshold' (1905), 'The Bird of Time' (1912), and 'The Broken Wing' (1917).

key_terms.definition: A nickname or descriptive name given to someone — Sarojini Naidu's sobriquet was 'The Nightingale of India'.
A young person who is exceptionally talented or intelligent for their age.
Having a musical, song-like quality — used to describe poetry that is melodious and rhythmic.

tables.title: Key Facts about Sarojini Naidu
rows: The Golden Threshold, The Bird of Time, The Broken Wing
Indian life, nature, beauty, joys and sorrows

fun_facts: Sarojini Naidu passed her matriculation at age 12 — she was truly a child prodigy!
She wrote the poem 'In the Bazaars of Hyderabad' about her own city — the very bazaars she grew up visiting.

sections.title: The Complete Poem — Text and Structure
sections.content:
Here is the complete poem for reference and study:

STANZA 1 — THE MERCHANTS:
What do you sell, O ye merchants?
Richly your wares are displayed,
Turbans of crimson and silver,
Tunics of purple brocade,
Mirrors with panels of amber,
Daggers with handles of jade.

STANZA 2 — VENDORS, MAIDENS, PEDLARS:
What do you weigh, O ye vendors?
Saffron, lentil, and rice.
What do you grind, O ye maidens?
Sandalwood, henna, and spice.
What do you call, O ye pedlars?
Chessmen and ivory dice.

STANZA 3 — THE GOLDSMITHS:
What do you make, O ye goldsmiths?
Wristlet and anklet and ring,
Bells for the feet of blue pigeons,
Frail as a dragon-fly's wing,
Girdles of gold for the dancers,
Scabbards of gold for the king.

STANZA 4 — FRUITMEN, MUSICIANS, MAGICIANS:
What do you cry, O fruitmen?
Citron, pomegranate, and plum.
What do you play, O ye musicians?
Sitar, Sarangi, and drums.
What do you chant, O ye magicians?
Spells for aeons to come.

STANZA 5 — THE FLOWER-GIRLS:
What do you weave, O ye flower-girls?
With tassels of azure and red?
Crowns for the brow of a bridegroom,
Chaplets to garland his bed,
Sheets of white blossoms new-garnered
To perfume the sleep of the dead.

STRUCTURE: The poem has 5 stanzas of 6 lines each (a total of 30 lines). Each stanza follows a question-and-answer format. The poet asks a question ('What do you sell/weigh/grind/make/cry/play/chant/weave?') and the tradespeople answer with a list of their goods or activities. This creates a rhythmic, musical quality — as if the poet is walking through the bazaar and talking to each group of workers.

RHYME SCHEME: Each stanza follows an AABCCB rhyme scheme (approximately). For example, in Stanza 1: merchants/displayed/silver/brocade/amber/jade — where 'displayed' rhymes with 'brocade' and 'amber' rhymes with 'jade'.

key_terms.definition: A group of lines in a poem, separated by a space from other groups — like a paragraph in prose.
The pattern of rhyming words at the end of each line in a poem, usually marked with letters (AABCCB).

fun_facts: The use of 'O ye' (meaning 'Oh, you') gives the poem an archaic, formal tone — as if the poet is addressing the workers with respect and admiration.
The poem mentions 9 different types of tradespeople — showing the incredible variety of an Indian bazaar!

sections.title: Stanza-by-Stanza Analysis: Stanza 1 — The Merchants
sections.content:
What do you sell, O ye merchants?
Richly your wares are displayed,
Turbans of crimson and silver,
Tunics of purple brocade,
Mirrors with panels of amber,
Daggers with handles of jade.

ANALYSIS: The poem opens with the speaker addressing the merchants — the cloth and goods sellers of the bazaar. The very first word 'Richly' sets the tone — these are not ordinary, dull goods. They are luxurious, colourful, and beautifully crafted.

The items listed appeal strongly to the sense of SIGHT:
- TURBANS of crimson and silver — deep red and gleaming silver headwear
- TUNICS of purple brocade — rich purple garments made of expensive woven fabric
- MIRRORS with panels of amber — looking glasses framed with yellow-brown gemstone
- DAGGERS with handles of jade — weapons decorated with green gemstone

Notice the colour palette: crimson (deep red), silver, purple, amber (golden-yellow), jade (green). The poet paints a vivid, colourful picture with just six lines.

The items also reveal the culture: turbans are traditionally worn in India, brocade is a symbol of wealth, and ornamental daggers reflect the Mughal and Nizam heritage of Hyderabad.

LITERARY DEVICE — Visual imagery: Every item in this stanza is described with a colour or material, creating a feast for the eyes.

key_terms.definition: Goods or merchandise for sale.
A deep, rich red colour.
An expensive, thick fabric with a raised pattern woven into it, often with gold or silver thread.
A hard, yellow-brown fossilised resin used for making jewellery and decorative objects.
A hard, typically green gemstone used for ornaments and jewellery.

fun_facts: Hyderabad was ruled by the Nizams for over 200 years. The bazaars of the old city (near Charminar) are still famous for pearls, brocade, and ornamental items — just like in the poem!
Brocade from Hyderabad (called Himroo fabric) is a traditional weave that dates back to the Mughal era.
examples: The colour scheme in this stanza — crimson, silver, purple, amber, jade — is like an artist's palette. Close your eyes and imagine walking into a market filled with these colours!

sections.title: Stanza-by-Stanza Analysis: Stanza 2 — Vendors, Maidens, Pedlars
sections.content:
What do you weigh, O ye vendors?
Saffron, lentil, and rice.
What do you grind, O ye maidens?
Sandalwood, henna, and spice.
What do you call, O ye pedlars?
Chessmen and ivory dice.

ANALYSIS: This stanza addresses three different groups in quick succession, each with a two-line question-and-answer. This rapid pace mimics the bustling energy of the bazaar — as you walk through, you encounter one seller after another.

THE VENDORS weigh saffron, lentil, and rice — everyday food essentials. Saffron is also the most expensive spice in the world, showing that even 'everyday' items in this bazaar have a touch of luxury.

THE MAIDENS grind sandalwood, henna, and spice — items associated with beauty, wedding rituals, and fragrance. This appeals to the sense of SMELL — imagine the aroma of freshly ground sandalwood and spices!

THE PEDLARS (travelling salesmen who carry their goods) call out chessmen and ivory dice — items of leisure and entertainment. Chess and dice are ancient Indian games.

The stanza moves from NECESSITY (food) to BEAUTY (cosmetics) to LEISURE (games) — showing that the bazaar caters to all aspects of life.

LITERARY DEVICES:
- Olfactory imagery (smell): sandalwood, henna, spice
- The archaic word 'ye' (meaning 'you') continues the formal, respectful tone
- Lists of three items (tricolon): 'saffron, lentil, and rice' — this rhythmic listing is used throughout the poem

key_terms.definition: People who sell goods, especially food and provisions.
A deep orange-yellow spice derived from the crocus flower — the most expensive spice in the world.
A reddish-brown dye made from the henna plant, used for decorating hands and colouring hair.
A person who travels from place to place selling small goods (also spelled 'peddler').

fun_facts: Saffron is so expensive because it takes about 150,000 crocus flowers to produce just 1 kg of saffron! It is sometimes called 'red gold'.
Chess originated in India (as 'Chaturanga') around the 6th century — so chessmen in Indian bazaars connect to a very long tradition.
examples: Think of T. Nagar market in Chennai or Laad Bazaar near Charminar in Hyderabad — you can find everything from spices to bangles to toys, just like in this poem.

sections.title: Stanza-by-Stanza Analysis: Stanza 3 — The Goldsmiths
sections.content:
What do you make, O ye goldsmiths?
Wristlet and anklet and ring,
Bells for the feet of blue pigeons,
Frail as a dragon-fly's wing,
Girdles of gold for the dancers,
Scabbards of gold for the king.

ANALYSIS: This stanza focuses entirely on the goldsmiths — perhaps the most skilled craftsmen in the bazaar. Their products range from the everyday to the extraordinary.

They make ordinary jewellery: wristlets (bracelets), anklets, and rings. But then the poem takes a beautiful turn — they also make tiny bells for the feet of blue pigeons! These bells are described as 'Frail as a dragon-fly's wing' — this is the poem's most famous SIMILE. The bells are so delicate, so light, so fragile that they are compared to the translucent, paper-thin wing of a dragonfly. It is a stunning image.

They also make girdles of gold for dancers (waist belts worn by classical dancers) and scabbards of gold for the king (decorative covers for royal swords). The progression moves from common jewellery → exquisite miniature craft → objects for dancers and royalty.

This stanza shows the incredible range and skill of Indian goldsmiths — from tiny pigeon bells to royal sword covers.

LITERARY DEVICES:
- SIMILE: 'Frail as a dragon-fly's wing' — compares the bells to dragonfly wings
- Visual and tactile imagery: you can picture the delicate golden bells
- Contrast: tiny pigeon bells vs. grand scabbards for the king

key_terms.definition: A bracelet or band worn around the wrist.
Delicate, fragile, easily broken — here describes the extreme lightness of the pigeon bells.
A belt or sash worn around the waist — here, golden waist belts for dancers.
A cover or sheath for the blade of a sword or dagger.

fun_facts: Putting tiny bells (called 'ghungroo') on pigeons' feet is a real tradition in Hyderabad! Pigeon-keeping (kabootarbaazi) has been a beloved hobby in the old city for centuries.
The 'Frail as a dragon-fly's wing' simile is considered one of the finest in Indian English poetry — examiners love asking about it!
examples: Think of how thin and see-through a dragonfly's wing is — now imagine a bell that light and delicate. That is the level of craftsmanship the poet is describing.

sections.title: Stanza-by-Stanza Analysis: Stanza 4 — Fruitmen, Musicians, Magicians
sections.content:
What do you cry, O fruitmen?
Citron, pomegranate, and plum.
What do you play, O ye musicians?
Sitar, Sarangi, and drums.
What do you chant, O ye magicians?
Spells for aeons to come.

ANALYSIS: Like Stanza 2, this stanza addresses three groups in quick succession. It captures the SOUNDS of the bazaar.

THE FRUITMEN 'cry' (call out) their fruits — citron (a large lemon-like fruit), pomegranate, and plum. 'Cry' here means to shout or call out their wares — a common sight in Indian markets where vendors loudly advertise their goods.

THE MUSICIANS play sitar, sarangi, and drums — three quintessentially Indian instruments. The sitar is a stringed instrument, the sarangi is a bowed string instrument with a hauntingly beautiful sound, and drums provide rhythm. This appeals to the sense of SOUND — you can almost hear the music.

THE MAGICIANS chant 'spells for aeons to come' — this is a mysterious, evocative line. 'Aeons' means extremely long periods of time. The magicians' spells are not ordinary tricks but ancient incantations meant to last forever. This adds an element of mystery and the supernatural to the bazaar.

The stanza moves from the physical (fruits) to the artistic (music) to the mystical (magic) — showing that the bazaar is not just a place of commerce but also of culture and wonder.

LITERARY DEVICES:
- Auditory imagery (sound): the cries of fruitmen, the music of instruments
- Hyperbole: 'spells for aeons to come' exaggerates the power of the magicians' spells
- Tricolon: three items listed each time ('citron, pomegranate, and plum')

key_terms.definition: A large, thick-skinned citrus fruit, similar to a lemon but larger and less sour.
A traditional Indian bowed string instrument known for its rich, emotional tone — often said to sound closest to the human voice.
An extremely long, indefinite period of time — often used to mean 'forever' or 'eternity'.

fun_facts: The sarangi is called the 'instrument that weeps' because its sound is so emotional and resembles the human voice!
Hyderabad has a long tradition of street magic and mystical performers — the poet captures this living tradition.
examples: Think of how fruit sellers call out 'Mangoes! Fresh mangoes!' in Chennai markets — that is exactly the 'cry' the poet describes.

sections.title: Stanza-by-Stanza Analysis: Stanza 5 — The Flower-Girls (The Final Twist)
sections.content:
What do you weave, O ye flower-girls?
With tassels of azure and red?
Crowns for the brow of a bridegroom,
Chaplets to garland his bed,
Sheets of white blossoms new-garnered
To perfume the sleep of the dead.

ANALYSIS: This is the most important and powerful stanza of the poem. It begins joyfully but ends with a haunting, sobering image.

The flower-girls weave with tassels (hanging threads or decorations) of azure (bright blue) and red — vivid, festive colours. They make crowns for the brow of a bridegroom — wedding decorations! They make chaplets (garlands) to garland his bed — celebrating marriage.

But then comes the dramatic twist in the last two lines: 'Sheets of white blossoms new-garnered / To perfume the sleep of the dead.' The flower-girls also weave freshly gathered white flowers for funerals — to cover and perfume the bodies of the deceased.

This ending is a MASTERSTROKE by Sarojini Naidu. It transforms the poem from a simple celebration of bazaar life into a profound meditation on the CYCLE OF LIFE AND DEATH. The same flower-girls, using the same skills, serve both the happiest occasion (a wedding) and the saddest (a death). The bazaar — and life itself — contains both joy and sorrow, beginnings and endings.

The phrase 'sleep of the dead' is a EUPHEMISM — a gentle way of referring to death, making it sound peaceful, like sleep.

Why is this ending so effective?
1. It is UNEXPECTED — after four stanzas of colourful, joyful imagery, the mention of death is shocking
2. It is UNIVERSAL — every person, rich or poor, experiences both weddings and funerals
3. It is BEAUTIFUL — even death is described with beauty (perfumed white blossoms)
4. It gives the poem DEPTH — without these last lines, the poem would be a nice but shallow description; with them, it becomes literature

key_terms.definition: Bright blue colour, like a clear sky.
A garland or circlet of flowers for a person's head.
Newly gathered or freshly collected.
A mild or indirect word used in place of something harsh or unpleasant — 'sleep of the dead' is a euphemism for death.

fun_facts: In Indian culture, white flowers (like jasmine and tuberose) are traditionally used both for weddings and funerals — the poet captures this dual use beautifully.
This final stanza is often considered the most exam-important part of the poem — expect Reference to Context questions from these lines!
examples: Think of a flower shop in Chennai — the same maalli (flower seller) strings jasmine for a bride's hair and also makes funeral garlands. This dual purpose of flowers is what the poet captures.

sections.title: Themes of the Poem
sections.content:
The poem explores several rich themes:

1. CULTURAL DIVERSITY AND RICHNESS OF INDIA: The bazaar is a microcosm (small representation) of India itself — full of colour, variety, craftsmanship, music, food, and tradition. The poem celebrates the incredible diversity of Indian culture through the goods sold in the marketplace.

2. TRADITIONAL INDIAN CRAFTSMANSHIP: Each group of workers — merchants, goldsmiths, maidens, flower-girls — represents generations of skill passed down through families. The poem is a tribute to the artisans of India.

3. SENSORY RICHNESS: The poem is a feast for the senses — you can SEE the colours (crimson, purple, azure), SMELL the sandalwood and spices, HEAR the music and cries of vendors, TOUCH the brocade and gold, and TASTE the fruits. This sensory richness celebrates the fullness of life.

4. THE CYCLE OF LIFE AND DEATH: The final stanza connects the joyful marketplace to the inevitable reality of death. The same bazaar that serves weddings also serves funerals. Life and death coexist in the same space.

5. COMMUNITY AND HARMONY: The bazaar brings together people from all walks of life — merchants, farmers, artists, musicians, magicians. They work side by side, serving different needs. The poem shows how a market reflects the harmonious diversity of Indian communities.

6. NATIONAL PRIDE AND IDENTITY: Written during the era of British colonial rule, this poem is also an assertion of Indian identity. By celebrating Indian goods (brocade, saffron, sitar, henna), Indian traditions (pigeon bells, bridal garlands), and Indian life, Naidu affirms the beauty and value of Indian culture.

key_terms.definition: A small representation of something much larger — the bazaar is a microcosm of Indian culture.
A skilled craftsperson who makes things by hand.

fun_facts: Sarojini Naidu wrote this poem during British colonial rule. By celebrating Indian markets (not British shops), she was making a subtle political statement about the value of indigenous Indian culture.
examples: The theme of cultural diversity is similar to walking through T. Nagar in Chennai during Deepavali — you see silk sarees, gold jewellery, sweets, firecrackers, and flower garlands all in one place.
The cycle of life and death theme is like how the same Sastha Catering prepares food for both weddings and remembrance ceremonies — the same hands serve joy and sorrow.

sections.title: Literary Devices in the Poem
sections.content:
Sarojini Naidu uses several literary devices that make this poem memorable:

1. IMAGERY (the dominant device): The poem is bursting with sensory images.
- VISUAL: crimson turbans, purple brocade, amber panels, jade handles, blue pigeons, azure tassels, white blossoms
- OLFACTORY (smell): sandalwood, henna, spice, perfumed blossoms
- AUDITORY (sound): cries of fruitmen, sitar and sarangi music, magicians' chants
- TACTILE (touch): brocade fabric, gold girdles, ivory dice
- GUSTATORY (taste): implied by fruits (citron, pomegranate, plum) and spices

2. SIMILE: 'Frail as a dragon-fly's wing' — compares the delicate pigeon bells to the thin, translucent wing of a dragonfly. This is the only simile in the poem but it is extraordinarily effective.

3. RHETORICAL QUESTIONS: Every stanza begins with a question ('What do you sell?', 'What do you weigh?', etc.). These are not real questions — the poet already sees the answers. They create a conversational tone, as if the poet is engaging directly with the bazaar workers.

4. REPETITION AND ANAPHORA: The pattern 'What do you..., O ye...?' is repeated throughout the poem. This repetition creates rhythm and musicality, mimicking the repetitive calls of vendors in a real bazaar.

5. LISTING (ENUMERATION): Each answer is a list of items ('saffron, lentil, and rice'; 'citron, pomegranate, and plum'). The lists create a sense of abundance and variety.

6. CONTRAST: The final stanza contrasts wedding garlands (joy) with funeral flowers (death). The colourful azure and red tassels contrast with the white blossoms for the dead.

7. EUPHEMISM: 'Sleep of the dead' — death is described as 'sleep,' making it sound peaceful and gentle.

8. ALLITERATION: 'purple brocade', 'panels... amber', 'blue... bells' — repetition of consonant sounds adds to the musical quality.

key_terms.definition: Descriptive language that appeals to the five senses (sight, sound, smell, touch, taste).
A comparison between two unlike things using 'as' or 'like' — e.g., 'frail AS a dragon-fly's wing'.
A question asked for effect, not to get an answer — the poet already knows what the bazaar workers sell.
Repetition of a word or phrase at the beginning of successive lines or clauses — 'What do you...' repeated throughout.
A mild, indirect expression used instead of a harsh or unpleasant one — 'sleep of the dead' for death.
Repetition of the same consonant sound at the beginning of nearby words — e.g., 'purple brocade'.

tables.title: Imagery — Sense-by-Sense Breakdown
rows: Crimson turbans, purple brocade, amber panels, jade handles, blue pigeons, azure tassels, white blossoms
Cries of fruitmen, sitar, sarangi, drums, magicians' chants
Brocade fabric, gold jewellery, ivory dice, flower garlands

fun_facts: The poem appeals to ALL FIVE senses — this is rare and is one reason why it is considered such a masterpiece of descriptive poetry.

sections.title: Important Vocabulary from the Poem
sections.content:
The poem is rich in vocabulary. Here are all the important words organised by category:

FABRICS AND CLOTHING:
- Wares: goods or merchandise for sale
- Brocade: expensive thick fabric with a woven pattern
- Turban: a traditional headwrap
- Tunic: a loose-fitting upper garment

GEMSTONES AND MATERIALS:
- Crimson: deep, rich red colour
- Amber: hard yellow-brown stone used for jewellery
- Jade: hard green gemstone
- Ivory: white material from elephant tusks (now illegal to trade)

JEWELLERY AND CRAFT:
- Wristlet: a bracelet
- Anklet: jewellery worn around the ankle
- Girdle: a belt or sash for the waist
- Scabbard: a sheath/cover for a sword or dagger

FOOD AND SPICES:
- Saffron: expensive orange-yellow spice
- Henna: reddish-brown dye from the henna plant
- Citron: a large lemon-like citrus fruit

MUSIC AND ENTERTAINMENT:
- Sarangi: a traditional Indian bowed string instrument
- Sitar: a large Indian stringed instrument
- Chessmen: chess pieces

FLOWERS AND CEREMONIES:
- Azure: bright blue
- Chaplet: a garland or circlet for the head
- New-garnered: freshly/newly gathered
- Tassels: hanging decorative threads

PEOPLE:
- Merchants: traders who sell goods
- Vendors: those who sell goods (especially food)
- Maidens: young women
- Pedlars: travelling salespeople (also 'peddler')

OTHER:
- Frail: delicate, fragile
- Aeon: an extremely long period of time
- Sobriquet: a nickname

sections.title: Foreign (Loan) Words in English — Vocabulary Exercise
sections.content:
The textbook includes a vocabulary exercise on FOREIGN WORDS used in English. The word 'bazaar' itself comes from Persian! English has borrowed words from many languages — Latin, French, Hindi, Arabic, Persian, and more.

Here are the foreign words from the textbook exercise with their meanings:

1. VERSUS (Latin) — against (e.g., India versus Australia)
2. ADIEU (French) — goodbye, farewell
3. BONHOMIE (French) — good-natured friendliness, warmth
4. GENTEEL (French) — elegant, refined, polite in manner
5. VERBATIM (Latin) — repeating the exact words someone said, word for word
6. EN MASSE (French) — all together, as a group
7. FAUX PAS (French) — an embarrassing action or remark, a social blunder
8. AD INTERIM (Latin) — temporarily, for the time being
9. INCOGNITO (Italian) — with a pretended/hidden identity, in disguise

The correct matching:
1-e (versus = against), 2-g (adieu = goodbye), 3-d (bonhomie = good-natured friendliness), 4-h (genteel = elegant, refined), 5-f (verbatim = repeating exact words), 6-i (en masse = all together), 7-c (faux pas = embarrassing action), 8-b (ad interim = temporarily), 9-a (incognito = pretended identity)

key_terms.definition: A word borrowed from another language and used in English — e.g., 'bazaar' from Persian, 'bonhomie' from French.
A French phrase meaning an embarrassing social mistake or blunder.
A Latin word meaning 'word for word' — repeating exactly what was said.

fun_facts: Many everyday English words come from Hindi/Urdu — 'jungle', 'loot', 'pyjamas', 'shampoo', 'verandah', 'bungalow' are all Indian words adopted into English!
'Bazaar' comes from the Persian word 'bāzār' meaning a marketplace. It entered English through trade with the Middle East and India.
examples: VERSUS: 'CSK versus MI in the IPL final.'
FAUX PAS: 'Calling your teacher by their first name would be a faux pas.'
INCOGNITO: 'The celebrity visited the bazaar incognito, wearing a disguise so nobody would recognise him.'

sections.title: Life Lessons and Exam Tips
sections.content:
LIFE LESSONS FROM THE POEM:

- Traditional markets are treasure houses of culture, not just places to buy things.
- Indian craftsmanship — from goldsmiths to weavers to musicians — is extraordinary and worth celebrating.
- Life and death are two sides of the same coin — the bazaar serves both weddings and funerals.
- There is beauty everywhere if you look closely — even in a busy, noisy marketplace.
- Diversity is a strength — the bazaar thrives because different people bring different skills.

EXAM TIPS FOR THIS POEM:

- The MOST IMPORTANT lines for exams are the last two: 'Sheets of white blossoms new-garnered / To perfume the sleep of the dead.' Know why they are significant.
- The SIMILE 'Frail as a dragon-fly's wing' is almost always asked about. Know what it compares (bells to dragonfly wings) and why it is effective (emphasises delicacy).
- For RTC questions: identify which STANZA the extract is from, WHO is being addressed, and WHAT items are mentioned.
- Know the FIVE SENSES and which images from the poem appeal to each sense — this is a common question.
- For 'appreciating the poem' questions: talk about imagery, simile, rhetorical questions, and the dramatic contrast in the final stanza.
- Know the VOCABULARY from the poem — wares, brocade, amber, jade, scabbard, citron, aeon, azure, chaplet, new-garnered.
- Know the FOREIGN WORDS matching exercise — versus, adieu, bonhomie, genteel, verbatim, en masse, faux pas, ad interim, incognito.
- For 'theme' questions: always mention BOTH the celebration of Indian culture AND the life-death contrast in the final stanza.
- The poet is SAROJINI NAIDU (not 'the speaker'). Since this is a first-person poem where the poet addresses bazaar workers directly, you can refer to her by name.

tables.title: Exam Focus Areas
rows: Identify stanza, who is addressed, items listed, significance
3-4 sentences with direct quotes from the poem
List examples for all 5 senses from the poem
'Frail as a dragon-fly's wing' — what, why, effect
Know meanings of wares, brocade, jade, scabbard, citron, aeon, azure, chaplet
Match all 9 words: versus, adieu, bonhomie, etc.
Wedding + funeral contrast, cycle of life and death

examples: If asked 'What is the significance of the last two lines?' — say: 'The last two lines — Sheets of white blossoms new-garnered / To perfume the sleep of the dead — provide a dramatic contrast to the rest of the poem. While the poem celebrates the colourful, joyful life of the bazaar, these lines remind us that the same marketplace also serves death. The flower-girls who make wedding garlands also make funeral flowers. This connects the vibrant bazaar to the universal cycle of life and death, giving the poem great depth and emotional power.'
